refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-005 Refined: The NGHE solution shall automatically derive performance requirements (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability, safety margins) from worksite mission context and operational parameters, record them in a traceable baseline, and maintain a managed history of all requirement updates and calibration changes with full audit trail.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR was vague on 'method' and lacked explicit traceability and history management. Refinement adds specificity: (1) automatic derivation from mission context, (2) explicit baseline recording, (3) managed history with audit trail. This aligns with acceptance criteria demanding documented performance targets traceable to worksite inputs and maintained baselines/calibration updates. High safety criticality demands explicit safety margin derivation and audit capability.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verification shall confirm: (a) performance requirements are automatically computed from worksite mission inputs (e.g., terrain, payload, cycle count); (b) baseline is recorded with timestamp, source, and derivation method; (c) history log captures all updates with reason, timestamp, and approver; (d) traceability chain from worksite input → derived requirement → baseline → history is complete and auditable; (e) safety margins are explicitly calculated and documented.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-005.1: Ingest worksite mission context (terrain, payload, cycle targets, safety constraints) from Site Manager input.
CR-NGHE-005.2: Compute performance requirements (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability, safety margins) using mission-context-driven algorithms.
CR-NGHE-005.3: Record derived performance requirements as a baseline with metadata (timestamp, source, derivation method, approver).
CR-NGHE-005.4: Maintain a managed history of all performance requirement baselines and calibration updates with full audit trail (who, what, when, why).
CR-NGHE-005.5: Provide traceability reporting linking worksite inputs → derived requirements → baselines → history.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Centralized Performance Derivation Engine – A dedicated software module that ingests worksite mission parameters, applies deterministic algorithms (physics-based cycle time, energy models, availability calculations), and outputs a structured performance requirement set. Baseline and history managed in a versioned configuration database with audit logging.

DP-B: Distributed Derivation with Consensus – Performance requirements derived collaboratively across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services modules, each contributing domain-specific constraints (e.g., Vehicle contributes mechanical limits, Safety Services contributes safety margins). Results merged and stored centrally.

DP-C: Template-Based Requirement Mapping – Pre-defined requirement templates matched against worksite mission context via lookup tables and heuristics. Minimal computation; fast baseline generation. History tracked via configuration management system.

DP-D: Operator-Guided Derivation with System Assistance – Site Manager and Tele-operator manually specify performance targets with system-provided suggestions based on mission context. System records baseline and manages history.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-A: Centralized Performance Derivation Engine with Versioned Configuration Database and Audit Logging

potential_sources: DP-A requires inputs from Vehicle (mechanical/energy limits) and Safety Services (safety constraints). These are external dependencies, not tight coupling within the derivation engine itself.
DP-A outputs performance requirements that feed into Vehicle operational planning and Safety Services monitoring. This is a clean, unidirectional dependency (derivation → consumption).
Audit logging and versioning are self-contained within the derivation engine; no circular dependencies.
coupling_check.notes: DP-A exhibits low coupling: it is a pure computation + storage module with well-defined inputs (mission context, constraints) and outputs (performance requirements, baselines, history). Dependencies are acyclic and can be managed via interface contracts.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Worksite mission context is available in a structured, machine-readable format (e.g., JSON, database record) at the time of requirement derivation.
ASSUMPTION: Vehicle and Safety Services can provide their constraint inputs (mechanical limits, safety margins) in a timely manner during derivation.
RISK: If mission context is incomplete or ambiguous, derivation may produce invalid or unsafe performance targets. Mitigation: Implement validation rules and fallback to conservative defaults.
RISK: Audit logging overhead may impact real-time responsiveness if history queries are frequent. Mitigation: Use asynchronous logging and indexed queries.
RISK: Versioning and baseline management complexity may grow with system lifetime. Mitigation: Implement retention policies and archive old baselines.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: DP-A is sound. Recommend adding explicit specification of: (1) the algorithm(s) used to derive each performance requirement type (cycle time, energy, availability, safety margins); (2) the schema for mission context input; (3) the baseline record structure and metadata; (4) the audit log format and retention policy. These should be detailed in the DP specification document.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution module is the top-level system integration boundary. It owns the Performance Derivation Engine (DP-A), including the derivation algorithms, baseline recording, history management, and audit logging. It also owns the interfaces to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services for constraint inputs and requirement distribution.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives worksite mission context (terrain, payload, cycle targets, safety constraints) from Site Manager via Tasking interface. This is the primary input to the Performance Derivation Engine.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Exchanges safety constraints and safety margin requirements with Safety Services. Inbound: safety constraints and limits. Outbound: derived safety margin requirements and audit trail of safety-critical baseline changes.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Outputs derived performance requirements, baselines, and history records to Vehicle and Teleoperation for operational planning and monitoring. Also receives Vehicle telemetry (actual cycle time, energy consumption, availability) for comparison against baselines.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Provides connectivity for remote access to performance requirement baselines and history by Site Manager, Maintenance/Quality, and System Engineer. Supports audit log queries and baseline retrieval.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the top-level system boundary and the natural owner of cross-cutting concerns like performance requirement derivation, baseline management, and audit logging. The derivation engine is a core system-level function that must be centralized to ensure consistency, traceability, and safety. Minimal diffusion: only the audit logging interface may be shared with other modules for compliance reporting.

Vehicle is a physical/operational module focused on mechanical execution and telemetry. While it provides constraint inputs (mechanical limits, energy capacity) to the derivation engine, it should NOT own the derivation logic itself. Placing DP-A in Vehicle would fragment the requirement derivation across multiple vehicles and create inconsistency. High diffusion risk.

Teleoperation is a remote control and monitoring interface. It consumes performance requirements but does not derive them. Placing DP-A in Teleoperation would create a dependency on remote connectivity for a critical system function. High diffusion risk and operational fragility.

Safety Services provides safety constraints and validates safety margins. While it is a stakeholder in performance requirement derivation, it should not own the entire derivation engine. Placing DP-A in Safety Services would create a safety-critical bottleneck and couple safety validation too tightly to requirement derivation. Recommend Safety Services own safety margin validation logic only, not the full derivation engine.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Performance Derivation Engine (DP-A) is best contained within the NGHE Solution module because: (1) it is a system-level function that must be centralized to ensure consistency and traceability across all vehicles and operations; (2) it integrates inputs from multiple stakeholders (Site Manager, Vehicle, Safety Services) and must maintain a single source of truth for baselines and history; (3) it is not a vehicle-specific, teleoperation-specific, or safety-specific function, but rather a cross-cutting system concern; (4) centralizing it in NGHE Solution enables audit logging, versioning, and compliance reporting at the system level.
containment_risks: RISK: If NGHE Solution becomes a monolithic module, the Performance Derivation Engine may be difficult to test, maintain, or evolve independently. MITIGATION: Implement DP-A as a well-defined, loosely-coupled subsystem within NGHE Solution with clear interfaces to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services.

RISK: If the derivation engine is tightly coupled to a specific database or configuration management system, it may be difficult to migrate or upgrade. MITIGATION: Use abstraction layers (e.g., repository pattern) to decouple the derivation logic from storage implementation.

RISK: If audit logging is not properly implemented, the system may fail to meet compliance and traceability requirements. MITIGATION: Implement audit logging as a first-class concern with explicit schema, retention policy, and query interface.

RISK: If the derivation engine is not properly validated, it may produce unsafe or infeasible performance targets. MITIGATION: Implement validation rules, constraint checking, and fallback to conservative defaults.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Assign DP-A to NGHE Solution with the following refinements: (1) Define DP-A as a subsystem within NGHE Solution with explicit ownership of the derivation algorithms, baseline database, and audit log. (2) Define clear interfaces between DP-A and Vehicle (constraint inputs), Safety Services (safety margin validation), and Teleoperation (requirement distribution). (3) Implement DP-A using a modular architecture (e.g., separate modules for cycle time derivation, energy efficiency derivation, availability derivation, safety margin derivation) to enable independent testing and evolution. (4) Specify the baseline record schema, audit log format, and retention policy. (5) Implement validation rules to ensure derived requirements are feasible and safe.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-A (Centralized Performance Derivation Engine) directly addresses all aspects of the refined FR: (1) it automatically derives performance requirements from worksite mission context; (2) it records baselines with full metadata; (3) it maintains a managed history with audit trail; (4) it enables traceability from worksite inputs to derived requirements. The DP is specific, implementable, and verifiable. No ambiguity or missing mappings.

diffusion_risks: DIFFUSION RISK (LOW): If the derivation algorithms are duplicated across Vehicle or Safety Services modules, consistency and maintainability will suffer. MITIGATION: Enforce single ownership in NGHE Solution and use interface contracts to prevent duplication.

DIFFUSION RISK (LOW): If audit logging is implemented separately in multiple modules, the audit trail will be fragmented. MITIGATION: Centralize audit logging in NGHE Solution and provide a unified query interface.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate container for DP-A. It is a system-level function that must be centralized to ensure consistency, traceability, and compliance. The module containment scores confirm this: NGHE Solution scores 95/100 for containment, while Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services score 20, 15, and 30 respectively. No diffusion risks are acceptable at this level.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: HIGH. The refined FR is verifiable through: (1) automated testing of derivation algorithms against known mission contexts; (2) inspection of baseline records for completeness and correctness; (3) audit log analysis for traceability and completeness; (4) integration testing with Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services to confirm requirement distribution and constraint integration. All verification activities are feasible within the NGHE Solution module.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: HIGH. The allocation of DP-A to NGHE Solution is clear and unambiguous. The external interfaces (Tasking, SafetyAlert, Telemetry, Network Link) are well-defined and map to known stakeholders and modules. The ownership boundary is explicit, and the scope of the module is well-understood.

recommended_revisions: REVISION 1: Add explicit specification of the derivation algorithms for each performance requirement type (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability, safety margins). Include mathematical models, assumptions, and validation rules.
REVISION 2: Define the mission context input schema (e.g., JSON schema or database schema) to ensure consistent data exchange between Site Manager and the Performance Derivation Engine.
REVISION 3: Specify the baseline record structure, including all required metadata (timestamp, source, derivation method, approver, version, safety margin, constraints used).
REVISION 4: Define the audit log format, retention policy, and query interface to support compliance reporting and traceability analysis.
REVISION 5: Specify the validation rules and constraint checking logic to ensure derived requirements are feasible and safe. Include fallback strategies for edge cases or missing inputs.

decision.justification: The refined FR is well-structured and decomposable into five atomic sub-FRs (CR-NGHE-005.1 through CR-NGHE-005.5). The selected DP (Centralized Performance Derivation Engine) is sound and passes the FR-DP coupling check. The assigned IM (NGHE Solution) is appropriate and passes the DP-IM containment check. All gate reviews pass. However, the FR is not yet atomic: it encompasses multiple distinct functions (ingestion, computation, recording, history management, traceability). To ensure complete and rigorous decomposition, we recommend proceeding to the next level to decompose each sub-FR into its own FR-DP-IM triplet. This will enable detailed design, implementation planning, and verification of each function.
decision.justification_detail: The refined FR CR-NGHE-005 is a composite requirement that spans five distinct functional areas: (1) mission context ingestion, (2) performance computation, (3) baseline recording, (4) history management, and (5) traceability reporting. While DP-A (Centralized Performance Derivation Engine) provides a unified solution concept, each sub-FR requires its own DP and IM assignment to ensure complete architectural clarity and implementation planning. Proceeding to the next level will decompose each sub-FR and assign specific DPs and IMs, enabling detailed design and verification.
next_level_sub_frs: CR-NGHE-005.1: Ingest worksite mission context (terrain, payload, cycle targets, safety constraints) from Site Manager input.
CR-NGHE-005.2: Compute performance requirements (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability, safety margins) using mission-context-driven algorithms.
CR-NGHE-005.3: Record derived performance requirements as a baseline with metadata (timestamp, source, derivation method, approver).
CR-NGHE-005.4: Maintain a managed history of all performance requirement baselines and calibration updates with full audit trail (who, what, when, why).
CR-NGHE-005.5: Provide traceability reporting linking worksite inputs → derived requirements → baselines → history.

assumptions: Worksite mission context is available in a structured, machine-readable format at the time of requirement derivation.
Vehicle and Safety Services can provide their constraint inputs (mechanical limits, safety margins) in a timely manner during derivation.
The Performance Derivation Engine is a system-level function that must be centralized in NGHE Solution to ensure consistency and traceability.
Audit logging and versioning are critical for compliance and must be implemented as first-class concerns.
The derivation algorithms are deterministic and can be validated against known mission contexts and expected outputs.
open_risks: If mission context is incomplete or ambiguous, derivation may produce invalid or unsafe performance targets. Mitigation: Implement validation rules and fallback to conservative defaults.

If audit logging is not properly implemented, the system may fail to meet compliance and traceability requirements. Mitigation: Implement audit logging as a first-class concern with explicit schema, retention policy, and query interface.

If the derivation engine is tightly coupled to a specific database or configuration management system, it may be difficult to migrate or upgrade. Mitigation: Use abstraction layers to decouple the derivation logic from storage implementation.

If the derivation algorithms are duplicated across Vehicle or Safety Services modules, consistency and maintainability will suffer. Mitigation: Enforce single ownership in NGHE Solution and use interface contracts to prevent duplication.
evidence_needed: Detailed specification of the derivation algorithms for each performance requirement type (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability, safety margins).
Mission context input schema (e.g., JSON schema or database schema).
Baseline record structure and metadata specification.
Audit log format, retention policy, and query interface specification.
Validation rules and constraint checking logic for derived requirements.
Integration test plan for DP-A with Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services.
Verification plan for traceability and audit trail completeness.
Safety analysis (FMEA or similar) for the Performance Derivation Engine to identify failure modes and mitigation strategies.
